transcription.subject: social_communication
transcription.raw_text:
Pragmatic Language Log - Lily - SLP: Ms. Tran
Date: 2026-04-10
---
Part A - Topic maintenance (3+ exchanges on peer's topic)
- Mia/soccer: 4 exchanges PASS
- Jordan/dogs: 3 exchanges PASS
- Group/field trip: monologue about octopuses FAIL
- Owen/Minecraft: 5 exchanges PASS
- Mia/family trip: 3 exchanges PASS
---
Part B - Emotion identification (5 photo cards)
- happy -> 'happy' PASS
- frustrated -> 'frustrated' PASS
- nervous -> 'nervous' PASS
- jealous -> 'mad' FAIL (close but not target)
- embarrassed -> 'embarrassed' PASS
---
Total: 8/10 = 80% - TARGET MET
Note: During math partner work, Lily noticed peer was frustrated and offered help unprompted. First time.
transcription.teacher_notes: Lily met the 80% pragmatic language target. Both sub-skills (topic maintenance AND emotion identification) are contributing. 1:1 conversations are solid — the only topic-maintenance failure was a larger group setting (field trip discussion), which is still her weakest context. The only emotion failure was 'jealous' called 'mad' — a close category error rather than a cluelessness. The most important qualitative data: during math partner work, Lily noticed her peer was frustrated and offered help unprompted. That is exactly the internalized-checklist moment the intervention was designed to produce.
engagement_indicators: self-initiated peer help on observed frustration
topic tracker tool working in 1:1 contexts

matched_goals.reasoning: SLP pragmatic language observation is the named measurement method for G1. 8/10 combined topic + emotion = 80% — exactly at target.

goal_mapping.overall_summary: Lily met the 80% pragmatic language target for the first time. Both sub-skills (topic maintenance and emotion recognition) are contributing. The internalized-checklist moment during math partner work is the qualitative proof that the skill is generalizing beyond the probe itself.

analyses.progress_note: Lily met her 80% pragmatic language target for the first time. Both halves of the goal contributed: topic maintenance in 1:1 conversations is now reliable (4 of 5 PASS), and emotion identification improved to 4 of 5 (only miss was 'jealous' read as 'mad' — a close-category error, not a cluelessness). The qualitative data point matters more than the percentage: during math partner work, Lily noticed her peer was frustrated and offered help unprompted. That is the exact generalization the intervention was designed to produce — the body-clue checklist moving from external scaffold to internalized skill. Group settings (more than 2 people) remain Lily's weakest context — her only topic-maintenance failure this session was the field trip group discussion, where she drifted back to octopuses. That is the next growth edge.
analyses.recommendation: Celebrate the math partner moment with Lily explicitly — she needs to hear that noticing her peer's frustration was exactly the skill we've been building. Next push: add targeted group-conversation probes (3+ people) since 1:1 mastery is holding. Consider reducing topic tracker scaffolding in 1:1 contexts to verify the skill holds without the visual prompt.
analyses.thinking:
Lily G1 progression (7 sessions):
1. 2026-02-07: 40% (baseline)
2. 2026-02-21: 50% (topic tracker introduced)
3. 2026-03-07: 60% (body clue checklist helping)
4. 2026-03-21: 65% (noticed peer upset at recess - first unprompted)
5. 2026-04-01: 70% (invited peer input in group project)
6. 2026-04-04: 75%
7. 2026-04-10 (today): 80% (TARGET MET)

Linear improvement across all 7 sessions, roughly 5-10 points per 2 weeks. The progression is unusually clean — every session shows growth or equal-to-previous, zero regressions. That's rare and means the intervention is well-matched to the goal.

Dual-component goal analysis: G1 has two independent sub-skills (topic maintenance, emotion identification from cues). Previous sessions, emotion identification was the bottleneck. This session, emotion identification hit 4/5 and topic maintenance held at 4/5. Both sub-skills have cleared the 80% threshold independently, which means the 80% aggregate isn't an artifact of one sub-skill carrying the other.

The math partner work incident is the highest-signal data point in Lily's entire trial history. It's an unprompted generalization to a novel context, without adult scaffolding, during an academic (not social-skills) task. That is the kind of data point a judge/parent/admin wants to hear about.

Confidence: high.
